title: Roast rib of beef on the bone
subtitle: Three-bone rib roast for a Sunday crowd
excerpt: A three-bone fore rib, the grandest British roast — seasoned, started at high heat, then cooked low to medium-rare. The bone conducts heat and adds flavour that a rolled joint lacks. One rib feeds two to three people. Rested, carved, and served with all the trimmings.
sourceNotes: Rib of beef on the bone is the British equivalent of the French côte de boeuf and the American prime rib. The fore rib provides the same eye of meat as a ribeye steak, surrounded by the rib-cap muscle, all attached to the bone. It is the roast for large gatherings — a three-bone joint weighs around 3 kg and serves six to eight people.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Season with salt the day before and leave uncovered in the fridge.
recipe.temperatureNote: 200°C fan for initial blast, then 160°C / 140°C fan

content.text: A three-bone joint (approximately 3 kg) is the right size for eight people. Ask the butcher to chine the bones — a cut along the spine that keeps them attached during roasting but allows easy carving. The scored bones double as a roasting rack, elevating the joint above the tin so fat drips down freely.

Use a meat thermometer. Times per kg are rough guides; thermometers are reliable. Target 55°C for medium-rare in the thickest part of the eye, away from the bone. The bone retains heat — rest for 30 minutes minimum, not 20.

items.prepNote: three-bone fore rib, at room temperature

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C. Rub the {{beef-rib}} all over with {{vegetable-oil}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Stand the joint bone-side down in a large roasting tin. Roast at 220°C for 20 minutes to sear the outside. Reduce to 160°C and continue roasting. For a 3 kg joint, allow approximately 15 minutes per 500 g after the initial blast for medium-rare. Use a meat thermometer: 50°C rare, 55°C medium-rare, 60°C medium. Remove 5°C before target. Rest on a warm board, covered loosely with foil, for at least 30 minutes. Carve by cutting down between the bones to release each rib, then slice the meat from the bone.

items.symptom: Uneven cooking — well done near the bone, rare in the centre
items.cause: Bone conducts heat differently to muscle
items.fix: Insert the thermometer at least 5 cm away from the bone for an accurate reading. The area right next to the bone will always be slightly different from the eye

items.cause: Oven temperature too low for the blast phase, or joint stored wet
items.fix: Pat the fat completely dry before seasoning and oiling. The initial 220°C blast should brown and crisp the fat cap noticeably

items.cause: Not chined or bones completely cooked off
items.fix: A chined joint stays together during cooking. If the bones are completely detached, carve the eye as a single piece rather than by rib

content.text: Rib of beef on the bone is the Sunday roast at its most ceremonial. It appears at Christmas, landmark birthdays, and any occasion where the host wants to signal that something important is being marked. The Ivy and The Savoy both featured it for decades as their signature Sunday joint. In France the same cut is côte de boeuf, grilled over charcoal; the British tradition roasts it slowly in the oven and serves it with all the classical trimmings.
